rationale: The citing paper uses Dhar and Simonson (2003) as the key prior evidence for how no-choice options reallocate choice shares across existing alternatives. It then explicitly positions its own contribution as moving beyond that focus on deferral/share loss to study how the mere availability of no-choice changes decision processes and preferences even among consumers who still choose an option. This is more than a passing mention because the cited paper helps define the immediate research gap and baseline scope of prior no-choice work.
relation_description: Parker and Schrift build on Dhar and Simonson's no-choice/share-shift findings by extending the inquiry to process-level consequences of rejectable versus forced choice sets. They examine new mechanisms (evaluative vs. comparative judgment, attribute weighting, threshold effects) rather than only which options lose share to no-choice.
